planting.note: Transplant purchased tubers or divisions in early spring before new growth emerges. Disturb roots as little as possible and plant at the same depth they were growing previously. These orchids depend on specific soil mycorrhizae, so transplanting into undisturbed woodland soil improves success.

directions: Plant in dappled shade at the edge of woodland or under deciduous trees where soil is moist but well-drained. Enrich the planting site with leaf mold rather than fertilizer, as these orchids thrive in low-nutrient conditions similar to their native meadow and open-woodland habitat. The characteristically spotted leaves emerge in early spring; protect plants from slugs during this vulnerable phase. Avoid disturbing established clumps, as the underground tubers are fragile and the mycorrhizal network they depend on is easily disrupted.

planting.note: Set out tubers or divisions in early spring into acidic, moisture-retentive soil. Amend heavy clay with coarse grit and leaf mold to improve drainage while retaining the consistent moisture this species demands. Avoid alkaline or heavily fertilized soils, which suppress the fungal partners these orchids require.

directions: Choose a site with acidic, peaty, or sandy soil that stays consistently moist without becoming waterlogged. This orchid thrives in open woodland, heathland edges, and boggy clearings where it receives bright indirect light through a partial canopy. Mulch lightly with pine needles or acidic leaf mold each autumn to maintain soil acidity and protect the shallow tubers from frost heave. Plants establish slowly and should not be moved once settled, as relocating them typically breaks the essential mycorrhizal associations.

planting.note: Transplant tubers in early spring into a site that mimics semi-shaded European woodland with neutral to slightly calcareous soil. Handle tubers gently to avoid bruising, and plant at the same depth as the original growing position. Consistent soil moisture through the first growing season is essential for establishment.

directions: Plant in partially shaded spots beneath deciduous trees where dappled sunlight reaches the forest floor, in neutral to slightly alkaline, well-drained but moisture-retentive soil. This orchid produces its sweetly vanilla-scented flower spikes in early summer and relies on night-flying moths for pollination, so undisturbed sites near hedgerows or woodland edges are ideal. Avoid over-mulching around the base, as this can smother the rosette of basal leaves that appear in spring. Established plants are long-lived and best left undisturbed; clumps should only be divided when visibly overcrowded.

planting.note: Introduce tubers to calcareous or chalk-based soils in open woodland or grassland edges in early spring. Soil pH should ideally be neutral to alkaline; amend acidic soils with horticultural lime before planting. Keep the planting area free of aggressive grass competition, which can crowd out young plants before they establish.

directions: Select a site with calcareous, well-drained soil and partial shade from an open woodland canopy or tall hedgerow. This robust orchid performs best where thin, alkaline soils discourage competitive rank grasses and allow the orchid to access sunlight at ground level. The fragrant greenish-white flower spikes appear in late spring to early summer and attract long-tongued moths after dark. Maintain steady soil moisture through summer, and remove any encroaching vegetation each autumn to prevent smothering the basal leaf rosette that overwinters at ground level.

planting.note: Transfer small divisions of the coral-like rhizome in early spring, taking care to keep a portion of the surrounding native soil attached to preserve active fungal partners. Plant in undisturbed forest duff beneath mature deciduous or coniferous trees where leaf litter accumulates naturally. Do not attempt to grow in sterile or amended potting mix; success depends entirely on intact mycorrhizal networks already present in the native soil.

directions: Establish divisions beneath a closed woodland canopy in rich, humus-laden soil where minimal light reaches the forest floor, as this plant produces no chlorophyll and gains all nutrition through its fungal symbiont. Because it never photosynthesizes, it is entirely invisible above ground for most of the year, emerging only as a leafless reddish-brown stem bearing small spotted flowers in late spring or summer. Mark planting locations carefully to avoid accidental disturbance during the dormant season. Supplemental watering is rarely needed where natural leaf litter maintains consistent soil moisture, but avoid flooding or allowing the soil to dry out completely.

planting.note: Plant corm divisions in early spring before the leafless flowering stalk emerges, or transplant in late summer after the flower has set seed and while the new leaf is just beginning to develop. Place corms at a depth of one to two inches in moist, humus-rich woodland soil in deep shade. Keep surrounding soil consistently moist through the first autumn as the winter leaf establishes.

directions: Grow in deep shade beneath mature deciduous trees in moist, rich woodland soil with a thick layer of decomposing leaf litter. This orchid follows an unusual seasonal rhythm: a single large, pleated, silver-veined leaf appears in late summer and persists through winter, dying back completely before the leafless flowering stalk rises in late spring bearing small, fragrant, yellow-green blooms. Plant where it will receive seasonal moisture from autumn rains and natural snowmelt, as it is adapted to the wet-winter, dry-summer cycle of the eastern deciduous forest understory. Allow fallen leaves to accumulate around plants each autumn rather than raking, as the organic layer is critical to the fungal associations that sustain the corm.
